html:
X

The Fall of the Emerald

I

As I think of that wretched night of the childrenвАЩs party there will be two pictures that cross my mind. The first, of a group of brightly coloured people, for we were in white flannels and the women in those mad, barbaric colours which fashion, goaded on by Chanel and gallantly led by Captain Molyneux, has lately flung as a challenge to our dark civilisation, around a table lit by the cameo flames rising from eight tall cast candlesticks by Paul Lamarie; and I remember that in the still air of GuyвАЩs great, bare diningroom those cameo flames never flickered even so much, they might have been flowers of light cut out of the stifling heat.

The second picture is of a darkness. A darkness torn here and there by the sudden flame of a match which drove the stars trembling back into the invisible and joined to groping eyes the silky soft blackness of the water. The black night pinned round the world with stars, shouts, laughter, splashings, an empty boat, silence, shrieks, a whisper from the black face of the water, and so home. Total losses: one stocking and one emerald. вАЬIвАЩm so glad, so glad,вАЭ she whispered, just before going to sleep against my shoulder, for it was Hugo Cypress who was riding the stork homeward.

But I have said that whilst we were on our way riverwards, and I sitting beside Iris as she hurled us headlong through the still night, we stood at enmity, she and IвБ†вАФfor Venice! And yet, so far as I could make out, there was not a soul but myself out of that party, Guy, Napier, Venice, Hugo, Shirley, Iris, who seemed in the least degree uncomfortable. Those people had been, throughout dinner and afterwards, completely and supremely normal. For all you knew, I mean, they might have been having fun. There werenвАЩt any undercurrents. Not even what you would call any undercurrents. Those people were quite calmly themselves, they just behaved as themselves in that confoundedly unassailable way which is peculiar to the people of this small island: as though, to be sure, they werenвАЩt giving away anything of a personal nature even to themselves. You canвАЩt help seeing why Napoleon found these people so detestable.

And it was all, you couldnвАЩt help feeling, so mean, such a humbug of a thing. I suppose, of course, that I was the only one besides Iris and Napier who knew of their departure together in three daysвАЩ time. вАЬI have always wanted,вАЭ she had said to me, вАЬto go to Rio, and then across the continent. One canвАЩt talk in Europe, itвАЩs got so stuffy now. But I always thought I would keep the Americas until my fate should be fulfilled.вАЭ Yet, I was quite certain, everyone at the table must have known that something was wrong, else why was that fell, beautiful lady there at all? For Guy, in the ordinary way, wouldnвАЩt, it simply wasnвАЩt in GuyвАЩs nature to be able to, ask Iris Storm to the same dinner with the young wives of his two young friends, his prot√©g√©s almost, Napier and Hugo. And if he had asked Iris tonight, knowing that she wouldnвАЩt funk comingвБ†вАФthough the real reason why she had come was that Iris simply did not attach any importance to such things, вАЬand besides,вАЭ she had said, вАЬI want to see dear Hugo again, and as a married manвАЭвБ†вАФit was just because he wanted Iris to realise the scene on which she was intruding so wickedly. It is such catholic cruelty as GuyвАЩs that, by always lopping off the rotten limb, has kept the heritage of so many English houses almost, despite the common talk of the day, unimpaired. Tonight he was wanting Iris to see that her old friends, her old playmates, Hugo and Napier, had grown up differently from herвБ†вАФbetter or worse, that wasnвАЩt GuyвАЩs point, but differentlyвБ†вАФthat while she had lived according to her nature they had lived according to their country, they and their young wives, Shirley and Venice. Not the most prejudiced eye, Guy knew, could but see that they made a fine, harmonious, clean four. Youth was there, and simplicity, and friendship, and love, too. And Guy had dared Iris to come to the childrenвАЩs party merely to say to her, with her eyes: вАЬSee, Iris, here are four people, two by two, happily paired, friends and lovers, husbands and wives. See, Iris, and let them be. One of these men you may be able to introduce to the magic mysteries of love more completely than his young wife ever can. But see, Iris, how much you deprive him of, how utterly you deprive her! You have put yourself outside this long ago, you never can be of this again. See, Iris, how happy they are, and young, and clean, and earnest to do right: most earnest to do right, Iris, despite the most damnable enchantments. And as they are so you might have been with Boy Fenwick, but you chose differently. Iris March, the death of Boy Fenwick puts you out of court. See, Iris, and for GodвАЩs sake let these children be!вАЭ

But that catholic Guy had not seen that beautiful white mask between the tawny formal curls and the two amethysts for eyes. I have told him since that had he seen that mask he would have foreseen the little profit he might expect to derive for his friends from IrisвАЩs presence at that dinner. I have told him how it was in my mind that night that nothing could move Iris, because it was as though in winning Napier she was winning the thirty yearsвАЩ war of her life. The shameless lady had at last lopped off the limb that was called the shameful lady; and so she had come again out of the darkness to Napier, she had come again as the enchanted voice whispering of better dreams, and not all GuyвАЩs Englishry could hold Napier now from following that enchanted whisper across the seas, that Iris March might at last come to fulfil her fate.

And yet, by the perfection of their normality over dinner, it might have been this person, me, who was being treacherous to his friends by fancying disloyalties among them! Shirley, for instance. Shirley, little sister to George Tarlyon, was of the same age as Venice, they had been at Heathfield together, they had always been together, and where Venice led there Shirley followed, and what Venice saw that Shirley saw, and where Venice raged there Shirley raged. And Venice was raging now. Oh, she must be raging frantically! Yet Shirley never once, as they say, вАЬlet onвАЭ about her state of mind. She was just Shirley all the time, sweet in a small way, sarcastic in a large way, Shirley of the brown eyes and unbreakable spirit, pretty Shirley. Maybe she was behaving a little better than was her general wont, for Shirley was so well-bred that she never practised what you would call deportment, but that was the only way the strain of that evening seemed to affect her.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

Exactly at what point, one wondered that evening, did behaviour become hypocrisy? For instance, Guy. There he sat, that knight of old beliefs, at our head, very gay in white flannels and a brilliant Fair Isle sweater, for all the world as though it was not already stifling enough, for all the world as though two people at his table hadnвАЩt offended him on the one essential point of conduct by which Guy de Travest knew friends from strangers: never to give way to what you want to do, if honour tells you that you may not do it.

And Napier, that love-lost man! Love-lost, that man? Let me tell of a moment after dinner when Venice suddenly, tremendously, helplessly, cried to Iris: вАЬOh, dear Jesu, arenвАЩt you lovely!вАЭ And Napier, at that moment gaiety itself, came suddenly between them, an arm round each of their shoulders. вАЬWhy, of course she is, Venice! I tell you, I was particular about my friends when I was young.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ It wasnвАЩt, of course, voluntary, he was not thinking, Napier couldnвАЩt think and then be a hypocrite: it was just the natural, normal sort of nonsense that happens. He had, at that moment, forgotten what he would have to tell Venice, tomorrow or the day after, of the love-philtre. And the child Venice! Venice, that very queen of hypocrites! Charming she was to Iris, just the tiniest bit deferential, as a girl of one-and-twenty might well be, but seldom is, to a woman of thirty. And yet Venice, ever since that afternoon in Paris, had been, I knew, eating her heart fretting about Napier, fearful and jealous and racked by what she could not see of his heart, tremulous with terror and suspicion of that legendary playmate, that Iris March of long ago. And how she hated the idea of Iris, I knew well, how she hated the thing she thought Iris wasвБ†вАФand wasnвАЩt Iris just that!вБ†вАФwith all the uncompromising savagery of her heart! Venice, O Venice! And once, over dinner, she whispered to me: вАЬI like Mrs.¬†Storm.вАЭ

I donвАЩt know, of course, but I suppose that in saying nothing one said quite enough to that.

вАЬI do really,вАЭ Venice insisted, but not with enough vehemence for one to be able to fix on that as evidence of her insincerity. вАЬShe gives you a sense ofвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ well, completeness, if you see what I mean?вАЭ

вАЬOh, quite,вАЭ I said. вАЬCompleteness, certainly.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬNot like Shirley and me, you see,вАЭ she said thoughtfully.

вАЬYes, I can just see that, Venice.вАЭ

вАЬMrs.¬†Storm,вАЭ said Venice gravely, вАЬgives one a sense of being a lady from herself, in her own right, if you see what I mean. Whereas Shirley and meвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬShirley and I.вАЭ

вАЬShirley and I, dear, and nearly everyone we know are ladies just because our mothers were, and that kind of thing. IвАЩd trust Mrs.¬†Storm.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

And so I was to tell Iris that Venice trusted her! And then, according to the Scriptures as written by Venice, Iris would feel such a cad that she wouldnвАЩt after all be able to bring herself to steal away her husband. She would repent, Iris would, on being told that Venice trusted her, and she would go back again into the nasty darkness of outlawry, leaving decent people to the safe enjoyment of their husbands. Dear Venice, I am afraid my telling Iris that wouldnвАЩt have quite that effect, she just wouldnвАЩt notice, Venice, that I had spoken; for such a plot might do exceeding well in a novel, whence you have no doubt derived it, but in life, Venice, your Iris March isnвАЩt to be deterred from her chase of the Blue Bird by being trusted. If only life was a movie, Venice, you would only have to let Iris know that you trusted her, and away she would slink, weeping.

But imagine that Venice, an eagle in her eyrie, desperately beating her wings to hide the sun from the eyes of her mate! Oh, but Venice acted superbly! Not, of course, that there wasnвАЩt provided a very handsome peg on which to hang the acting. The bathing idea came as a boon and a blessing to all the company. You could talk about an idea like that, and no harm done. Guy chivalrously gave me the credit for it, and I was acclaimed by Shirley and Venice as something they have in America called, so Venice swore on oath, a вАЬhe-man.вАЭ But Shirley thought it was very silly of Guy to go and spoil the whole picnic by insisting on bathing-costumes. Shirley thought that at length. So did Venice.

вАЬI mean, on a hearty picnic like this!вАЭ said Shirley helplessly. And Venice said it was absurd to go digging about among bathing-costumes on a nice, warm, pitch-black night. OneвАЩs chemise, said Shirley, would do ever so well. OneвАЩs chemise, said Venice, had done very well before. And one wouldnвАЩt, said Shirley, indignantly, really need the chemise afterwards, just to come home with. Not in this heat, said Venice, and they appealed to Iris, but Iris protested that she must be neutral, because she was not going to bathe; but she would have thought, she said, that a dry shift was always preferable, when possible.

вАЬNot going to bathe!вАЭ cried Venice. вАЬNot going toвБ†вАФOh, you must bathe! Of course youвАЩre going to bathe! Oi, youвАЩll spoil the whole party!вАЭ

It was after dinner, and Hugo was doing a few card-tricks with champagne-glasses, the idea being, Hugo said, to settle our digestions after one of the best dinners that had ever left him with an appetite.

вАЬOf course sheвАЩll bathe!вАЭ said Hugo. вАЬIвАЩve known the girl all my life, and IвАЩll answer for her. SheвАЩll bathe. Leave her to me. Silly, not bathing.вАЭ

вАЬSheвАЩs rather common, your friend,вАЭ Shirley sighed to Guy.

вАЬSickening. Cannot bathe, really, on a night like this.вАЭ

вАЬSeems to me,вАЭ Napier scowled, вАЬthat she will have to bathe. Tell me if IвАЩm wrong. What?вАЭ

вАЬListen,вАЭ Iris pleaded.

вАЬComing here,вАЭ said Shirley indignantly, вАЬand not bathing!вАЭ

вАЬI am terrified,вАЭ said Iris desperately. вАЬTerrified of masses of water. Once, in the Black Sea of all places, I got cramp, and ever since.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬIf you only knew,вАЭ sighed Hugo, вАЬhow cold all that leaves us! YouвАЩll swim, girl. Good for you. Make your coat shine. Give you back your lost youth.вАЭ

вАЬHugo, donвАЩt be so tactless!вАЭ cried Shirley.

вАЬThe girlвАЩs right,вАЭ Guy closed the discussion. вАЬSheвАЩs only been out of bed about a month.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬBut I havenвАЩt been near mine for longer than that!вАЭ cried Shirley inevitably, and it was just at that moment, under cover of it, that I touched the ice-cold hand. That was the only sign until we reached the river that VeniceвАЩs married life had tumbled like a house of cards about her heart, that and her вАЬtrustingвАЭ Iris.

Venice was saying: вАЬAnd didnвАЩt we just have some trouble with you, Mrs.¬†Storm, when you were ill in Paris! Naps white in the face thinking you were going to die, me green in the face thinking my holiday would be spoilt if you did, this he-man here purple in the face telling me to be reasonable.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬBut you were in bed for ages, werenвАЩt you?вАЭ said Shirley sympathetically. вАЬWhat was it? Some foul plague?вАЭ

вАЬPtomaine poisoning,вАЭ said Napier, and as I was giving Venice a light with which to torture yet another cigarette my hand happened to touch hers. вАЬIn this heat!вАЭ said I.

вАЬShut up, you fool!вАЭ she whispered desperately, and then she tried not to smile frantically, whispering: вАЬDarling, darling, darling! My one friend.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬVenice, theyвАЩre never any real good, friends. They canвАЩt do anything.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬI know. Oh, I know. Oh God, I know!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Mrs.¬†Storm, what a divine lipstick! May I see? May I use?вАЭ

Baby.

II

Thus, the childrenвАЩs party.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

Their engines no louder than a whisper through the quiet noises of the night, and swift as arrows with flaming eyes, two touring-cars, a primrose and a blue, passed through the villages riverwards. The good people slept on undisturbed, as why should they not, for a motorcar will disturb the amenities of a village by night less than a wheelbarrow. Maybe through the crack of a blind flashed a startling light on a sleepless pillow. Maybe a distant scream, as of a great seabird, stirred a boy to dream of vain, polite, perishable delights. Maybe a cow stared thoughtfully at the strange, swift, whirring insects with the livid eyes and the cruel screams. Here and there the lamps shone on the buttons of a policeman, stock-still in a doorway. There was no air but the wind of our passage, warm, heavy with dust and dry grasses. вАЬRain, rain!вАЭ breathed England in her sleep. And there was no rain, nor breath of rain, nor yet that damp, oppressive foretaste of a thunderstorm to come, only a torment of heat over the land and around the land the unclouded darkness pinned with faint stars. A myriad flies withstood the stork, were appalled, died. Wrapped in silence, armed with light, we fled beneath the suns of the night like battle-chariots rushing to the assault of the stronghold of the gods. Iris had gone mad.

I thought of Mr.¬†Polly disturbed in his sleep, twenty years ago, on a Sussex hayrick by the roar of a racing-car. Mr.¬†Polly could have slept undisturbed for us. One hundred and twenty horses drew us, shadows of nothing from nothing to nothing beneath the impersonal stare of the stars. Look away from the stars, lovers of the worldвАЩs delights, for they are the destroyers of the worldвАЩs delights with their dreams of grander things. To listen to great music, to adore God in vast solitudes, to kneel before the face of beauty, to pass through the quiet land like an arrow with flaming eyes, swifter than your thoughts: such and the like, according to each our nature, are the captains of the worldвАЩs delights, so keep your eyes from the stars, that destroy our delights with their dreams of grander things.

Silence marches with the thoughts in your mind. Maybe a word or two will drop, hesitate in the wind, fight with the dying hosts of midgets, perish on the road. Small flying things brush by your face, and a dry unsweet scent, as though England is sleeping with her windows closed.

The green hat was somewhere beside me, it fell and rolled about my feet, she murmured: вАЬLeave it.вАЭ To the warm wind fell the honour of the dance, and with the tawny cornstalks the wind stepped a wide-flung dance. Why does your hair dance so, Iris March, like a halo possessed of devils? Why this, why that, Iris March?

In the glass of the windscreen we might now and then see the faint reflection of GuyвАЩs lamps behind us. Nay, once or twice his bonnet nosed up beside Iris, just beside her elbow. But the stork cried hoarsely, flew on.

Again, silent as the rustle of a womanвАЩs dress walking in a dark garden, GuyвАЩs shining bonnet menaced the tail of our eye, and Guy himself, alone in front, yellow-haired, grim, fair herald of a fighting pageant in his brilliant Fair Isle sweater, and now the face of Venice, leaning forward to GuyвАЩs shoulder, excited, exhorting. Venice, for Venice! She would pass the lady of the dancing hair, would Venice. But the stork cried hoarsely, flew on.

We wrestled. Silent as phantoms, we wrestled. One hundred and twenty horses, a winged Mercury and a stork wrestled for the dominion of nothing on the Reading road.

There was a corner, proud and saturnine from many fell triumphs. The stork screamed a taunt, flew on.

вАЬHo!вАЭ gasped Hugo, chattering, from behind. вАЬSteady, girl! ShirleyвАЩs frightened.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬLet him pass, Iris!вАЭ cried I. A little scared, a woman driving, you never know, might lose her head, boyвАЩs head, curly head, white and tiger-tawny, but too white, too intent, too infernally reckless.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬIris, Iris!вАЭ

вАЬCan do seventy-five, if you like,вАЭ cried the lips of the dancing hair.

вАЬLet him pass, Iris!вАЭ

вАЬPass? Am I mad! As soon let happiness pass! See, the stars are laughing.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬIris, Iris!вАЭ

вАЬLet him pass, Iris! Damn you, it wonвАЩt hold the road!вАЭ

вАЬWhy, the roadвАЩs fainting with joy! Can do seventy-six if you like. But not more.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

A new road, recently laid down to soften the passage of footlight-favourites to the reaches of Taplow and Maidenhead, wide, deserted of houses. Meadows swept each side into the desert darkness. Iris, perhaps remembering Mr.¬†Polly, perhaps thinking Mr.¬†Polly had slept long enough, kicked open the exhaust. That lends another mile an hour to speed. Another sixty horses gave answer behind, then fell snarling back towards London. вАЬSeventy-one, Iris!вАЭ

вАЬOw!вАЭ she breathed. вАЬAccelerator burning foot. Ow! Hell!вАЭ

вАЬMaidenhead!вАЭ screamed Shirley.

вАЬTo the right, Iris!вАЭ

And so we came into the yard of QuindleвАЩs. Still, sleeping, shuttered, QuindleвАЩs hostelry was a rebuke to the flaming lights which made a festival of the desert scene. Then GuyвАЩs car swung in, poor winged Mercury. Shows one, donвАЩt you know, how much gods are worth.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬSickening, Iris. You had me properly beat that time.вАЭ

вАЬBut how my foot burns, Guy!вАЭ

вАЬLook!вАЭ said Venice. вАЬHist!вАЭ

A man in shirtsleeves was come out of the hotel. He stared at us, rubbed his eyes, stared at us.

вАЬHo!вАЭ called Major Cypress. вАЬHo, there! Is that QuindleвАЩs speaking?вАЭ

The man in shirtsleeves came through the flame of the lamps. An amiable man, he looked.

вАЬNow remember,вАЭ whispered Shirley at large, вАЬno matter how beastly they are to us, we are going to bathe. Let everyone speak at once. That will baffle him.вАЭ

вАЬEvening,вАЭ said the man in shirtsleeves. вАЬBit late, isnвАЩt it?вАЭ

вАЬNot one yet,вАЭ said Hugo. вАЬI say, we want to bathe.вАЭ

вАЬCanвАЩt have no rooms,вАЭ said the man in shirtsleeves. вАЬHotelвАЩs full.вАЭ

вАЬBut we donвАЩt want no rooms!вАЭ Venice pleaded. вАЬWe only want to bathe.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬBarвАЩs closed,вАЭ said the man in shirtsleeves.

вАЬServe you right,вАЭ said Hugo. вАЬBut weвАЩll give you a drink if you want one. Here you are. Beer or champagne?вАЭ

вАЬI want to bathe,вАЭ Shirley pleaded.

вАЬCanвАЩt bathe вАЩere,вАЭ said the man in shirtsleeves.

вАЬYou donвАЩt know about us,вАЭ said Venice severely. вАЬWe can bath anywhere.вАЭ

вАЬAgainst the lor, miss.вАЭ

вАЬThat will be all right about the law.вАЭ A sudden voice, a calm voice, a cold, chill murmur. It fell from heights like a douche. The man in shirtsleeves tried not to have to look up all the way to GuyвАЩs face. Too tall was Guy, in that light. Guy smiled down at the man in shirtsleeves.

вАЬHot night,вАЭ Guy murmured. вАЬVery hot. My children, all these.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬHo,вАЭ said the man in shirtsleeves. вАЬвАКвАЩOt or cold, itвАЩs against the lor, thatвАЩs wot.вАЭ

вАЬDonвАЩt you worry your head about the law,вАЭ said Guy. вАЬBut what you might do, now, would be to get us some towels. We forgot towels.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬAgainst the lor, anyhow,вАЭ said the man in shirtsleeves.

вАЬI do wish youвАЩd say something else just once,вАЭ snapped Shirley.

Iris, a white face, gardenia-white, mocking hair, a barbaric scarf about her throat, her hat a splash of black against the frail fancy that was her dress, standing a little away, staring at the stars. вАЬA light,вАЭ she murmured. вАЬA light!вАЭ Then Napier was beside her, lighting her cigarette, lighting also the curious, still smile on his acolyteвАЩs face, an enchanted smile, the smile of a man drowned in a magic pool. The collar of his white shirt was unbuttoned, the dark hair sleek in the glow of the flaming lights.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬNaps, give me a light, too.вАЭ

вАЬHere you are, Venice,вАЭ said Hugo.

вАЬOh, itвАЩs gone out! Naps, a light!вАЭ

вАЬSorry, Venice.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Guy seemed to be shaking hands with the man in shirtsleeves.

вАЬGet you some towels,вАЭ said he, moving off.

Hugo whispered: вАЬOne law for the rich, one for the poor. Dear me!вАЭ

вАЬSickening. But we may as well take advantage of whatвАЩs left of it. Getting a bit mouldy, that law.вАЭ

вАЬCome on, Hugo. God, itвАЩs dark! Which way is it? We must find a boat.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬNaps, this way! No, down hereвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ but hang on to my arm! Soon find a boat.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬYou canвАЩt have no boat!вАЭ called the man in shirtsleeves.

вАЬYou get those towels,вАЭ said Hugo severely. вАЬThe way you talk!вАЭ

вАЬPlease, your arm,вАЭ Iris begged me, husky voice. вАЬFoot hurts. And isnвАЩt it dark!вАЭ

вАЬHere you are!вАЭ came VeniceвАЩs clear boyвАЩs voice from the pit of darkness ahead, beneath us.

We faltered, blind as bats, down the slope of a landing-stage.

вАЬMatches, please!вАЭ ShirleyвАЩs voice. Oh, trust Shirley and Venice to have the affair well in hand! The pit of darkness ahead was bitten by tiny flames. вАЬOh, look out, Naps! Ow, God damn you!вАЭ

вАЬNaps, you might wipe your feet on your own wife, would you?вАЭ

There were uncertainties, holes, fissures of splintered wood. The tiny flames in the pit ahead were like lance-points thrusting the darkness deeper into the eyes.

Iris and I marched slowly as the smoke of our cigarettes in the breathless night. She leaned on my arm, completely. вАЬFoot hurts.вАЭ I wished she wouldnвАЩt. I almost said, вАЬdonвАЩt.вАЭ Her touch confounded, confused. She was tangible, until she touched you. She was finite, until she touched you. She was a woman, until she touched you. Then she became woman, and you water. She became a breath of womanhood clothed in the soft, delicious mystery of the flesh. Touching her, you touched all desire. She was impersonal and infinite, like all desire. She was indifferent to all but her desire, like all desire. She was a breath carved in flesh, like all desire. She was the flower of the plant of all desire. Desire is the name of the plant that Lilith sowed, and every now and then it puts out the flower that in the choir of flowers is the paramour of the mandrake.

вАЬYou are very silent, Iris.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬYesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ yes? Sometimes.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I donвАЩt know, but itвАЩs as though the stars make me nervous, sometimes. TheyвАЩre so hopeless. They sneer, I canвАЩt help thinking. But are we going right?вАЭ

The darkness ahead stirred with tiny flames and exultant voices. Venice and Shirley!

вАЬI say, lovely boat!вАЭ cried Shirley.

вАЬWhere, Shirley?вАЭ I called.

вАЬBetween you and me,вАЭ Iris whispered, вАЬI wouldnвАЩt mind sitting. Foot hurts.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬCome straight on. DonвАЩt go right or left. River.вАЭ

вАЬItвАЩs not a boat at all!вАЭ cried Venice. вАЬItвАЩs a lovely motor-canoe. Oh, chaps!вАЭ

вАЬSsh!вАЭ GuyвАЩs voice.

вАЬNaps, this way! Here you are! IsnвАЩt it a beauty?вАЭ

вАЬEveryoneвАЩs cold sober.вАЭ

вАЬSensible Guy!вАЭ

Our eyes pricked by the wicked little match-lights, we could just make out at our feet the shape of a long motor-canoe and, at one end of it, a jumble of figures. They seemed to be fighting, those figures, bent this way and that in heroic attitudes. The canoe twisted and rocked frantically on its moorings. Fierce whispers, wicked words.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬSteady a moment,вАЭ said Guy, just beside us. But they werenвАЩt steady any moments, Venice and Shirley and Hugo, whilst Napier helped them by getting in their way. They were up to something, those frantic figures.

вАЬSteady, I said!вАЭ said Guy sharply. That learnt them. Someone in the boat lit a match, and the water shone like black silk. I saw NapierвАЩs white face looking towards us, white face, dark eyes. Love-lost, dreaming.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬNow look here,вАЭ said Guy gently. вАЬJust leave those sickening ropes alone. You, Venice, you!вАЭ

вАЬMust nothing. ItвАЩs not our boat, Venice, and I never break more than one law a night.вАЭ

вАЬButвБ†вАФOh, damn the man!вАЭ

вАЬвАКвАЩEreвАЩs towels,вАЭ said a miserable voice.

The canoe rocked beneath us. At our end soft things dropped to our feet, got in the way. Never was so dark and still a night. It was a relief taking off even the white flannels.

вАЬAny swimming to be done,вАЭ said Guy, вАЬto be done in a straight line between this and the other bank. First man or woman who disobeys gets a crack on the head (a) from the bridge over there and (b) from me.вАЭ

вАЬI got one foot in the water,вАЭ she said at large.

The canoe rocked frantically. вАЬDamn you, Guy!вАЭ said Napier. The constable turned his bullвАЩs-eye to where he thought GuyвАЩs face would be, then flashed it a foot higher.

вАЬAbout this law,вАЭ murmured Guy.

вАЬThe very word! I was just going to ask you if it would be troubling you too much to ask you to run up to your house to lend us some towels. It really would be very kind of you. Our friend here hasnвАЩt brought us quite enough.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Splash!

вАЬLook вАЩere,вАЭ began the Voice desperately.

вАЬDonвАЩt look, constable! Be strong. Use your willpower. Women are but idle vanities.вАЭ

The river was warm, soft, quiet. Most un-English were the waters of the Thames that night, most Italianate. Never before had one understood the verity of that phrase вАЬon the bosom of the waters.вАЭ

From several yards away I could see the long shape of the motor-canoe. How LamornaвАЩs creditors would like to hear of that canoe! Hugo would blackmail him for his fiver. Dear Hugo. Suddenly the glow of IrisвАЩs cigarette stabbed the darkness, and maybe that was her shadow there, and that the one foot in the water.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬWhoвАЩs that?вАЭ she gasped.

I was anchored to her ankle. My hand could have gone twice round it.

вАЬTake care of them,вАЭ she whispered. вАЬDear, take care of them. Keep your eyes on that Venice child. SheвАЩs reckless. Quick, and catch them up. I rely on you somehowвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬYou mustnвАЩt, Iris. I am enemy to Iris Storm.вАЭ

вАЬOh, friends and enemies! One relies on what people are in themselves, no matter what circumstances may make them feel.вАЭ

вАЬAnd circumstances, IrisвБ†вАФdo they make a woman so heartless?вАЭ

вАЬHeartless! ThatвАЩs a large word, rather. Heartless? But maybe I am tired of being unhappy. So maybe I walked into a garden and built a high wall round it. Oh, may be, may be! Dear friend, go after them now. I am nervous, theyвАЩre so young. By their voices, they seem to have gone very far.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

But from the water the voices seemed to come from within a foot of oneвАЩs ear. They must, I thought, be straight ahead, towards the opposite bank. Swiftly a whisper cut the water near me, past me. вАЬYoung slacker!вАЭ came GuyвАЩs murmur. But I, not for exercise was I on the bosom of the waters that night. I lazed, listening to the voices ahead, sharp and clear across the water. Dimly, softly, clammy-cold, a weed would brush one. The stars were like the lance-points of a mighty host marching down to the chastisement of the world. But the darkness baffled them, whilst I floated into the heart of it, I loitered.

вАЬMind your head on this quay here, Venice! Venice! Hello, whereвАЩs Venice?вАЭ

вАЬHere. I say, whatвАЩs this place?вАЭ

вАЬOh, my pretty dears, why isnвАЩt one always in the water! I say, whatвАЩs this wooden thing?вАЭ

вАЬLooks like a landing-stage to me. What? I say, Hugo, whatвАЩs this place? What?вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩve heard thereвАЩs a River-Night-Club arrangement about here. Very exclusive.вАЭ

вАЬWe know. Excludes all who canвАЩt crowd in. Come on. Me for wine.вАЭ

I found them, having almost broken my shins against a wooden affair, lying grouped on what Shirley said was unmistakably вАЬa sweep of velvet sward.вАЭ Venice, it seemed, was exploring. You couldnвАЩt see your hand before your face. But you didnвАЩt want to.

вАЬFunny,вАЭ sighed Hugo, вАЬif chap, just any chap, probably quite a nice chap, but timid chap, wakes from sleep to see Venice looking in on him. Mermaid theory.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

I think that Guy and Hugo and Shirley must have deflected rather to one side, for although I was the poorest swimmer of the four I arrived first at the boat. My sight in the darkness was not helped by bumping my head against the gunwale.

Napier did not call on IrisвАЩs name. Hugo chattered to cover the silence. I thought I heard Guy mutter something between his teeth. During the next few minutes HugoвАЩs dexterity with the champagne was a great relief. Dear Hugo.

вАЬVenice!вАЭ said Guy beside me, chill, queerly harsh. вАЬYour health, Venice! YouвАЩll need a good deal, if you go playing any more tricks like that.вАЭ

вАЬOhвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ no, itвАЩs quite all right, really. Please, really. But would you mind seeing if my shoes are anywhere there, by the steering-wheel thing?вАЭ

Formal, like the voices of women in a drawing-room.

Iris called to me for a cigarette. It was her right hand to which I gave it. It seemed very naked, that right hand. вАЬYour ring, Iris?вАЭ

вАЬIn the Thames,вАЭ she whispered. вАЬFallen forever! Not a word.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Venice was explaining to the darkness, gulping lavishly at her champagne: вАЬThought IвАЩd go for a swim and not just paddle about. Thought IвАЩd be clever. Thought you were fools. Thought IвАЩd thought right. Thought IвАЩdвБ†вАФanyhow, I caught my head crack (a) on that bridge. And then I didnвАЩt want to let out a yell about nothing and look a silly ass. Heard you calling me, but thought IвАЩd better keep my breath for swimming. Began swimming, and got a weed like a wrestlerвАЩs torso round me. Head hurt, like hell it did. Thinks I, now for a yell, but began kicking insteadвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬYou would!вАЭ

вАЬWait. And my head hurt. And I was frightened to death. And I prayed like fury. Naps! WhereвАЩs Naps? I missed you. And when I wanted to yell all I could let out was a miow. And Mrs.¬†StormвБ†вАФwell, Iris, as she saved my life, cries out: вАШOi, whatвАЩs that? Who? Where?вАЩ And before you could say knife, and just as I was succumbing to a watery grave, she was saving my life, quick as you like. Quick, terse stuff. вАЬGet very easily tired,вАЭ Iris said.

вАЬIвАЩm almost ready, Napier.вАЭ Impatient, IrisвАЩs voice was, I thought.

вАЬNaps, get a rug from the car. SheвАЩs shivering.вАЭ

вАЬPlease!вАЭ Iris whispered, frantically, desperately. вАЬFor pityвАЩs sake, please not!вАЭ

Silence.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

As we collected round the two motorcars, Guy, fiddling about with his starting-arrangements, seemed, I thought, to be saying something. But he was only swearing.

At the back of the Hispano Iris went to sleep against my shoulder. She spoke in her sleep: вАЬYou will find me quite light on you, as I havenвАЩt got a chemise. They say it is very smart, to be chemiseless. Already I feel less of an outlaw from society. She did it on purpose.вАЭ

вАЬIris!вАЭ

вАЬShe did. Half on purpose. I know she did. The pet! Oh, dear.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬBut, Iris, why?вАЭ

вАЬBecause, dear. So that I should like her.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬOh! Well, do you?вАЭ

вАЬYes. Oh, yes.вАЭ

вАЬWell?вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm sorry if my hair is tickling your face.вАЭ

вАЬWell, now you like her, does it make any difference?вАЭ

вАЬNo. Oh, no.вАЭ

вАЬOh!вАЭ

вАЬGood night.вАЭ
